meta.title: When Internet First appeared in Ukraine?
meta.source_file: When Internet First appeared in Ukraine.docx

content.text: When Internet First appeared in Ukraine?
First appearance

As with everything “foreign” - like toilet paper or washing machines - Ukraine learned about the internet quite late, roughly 20 years after it was introduced globally. But actually,  the idea and so the need to create unified system of computers in the former USSR was discussed as early as in the 80s, but was discarded as Soviet Union was busy with supposedly “more important” matters (economic, political, social and environment instability and crises).

With all of that events happening in post-USSR countries only in 1990 Yurii Yankovskyi(Юрій Янковський, pioneer of Ukrainian internet, more here: https://www.radiosvoboda.org/a/24908485.html, unfortunately on Ukrainian language but still pretty interesting to hear how it was done with thought “why not?” as it wasn’t country initiative) was the first to connect to network. More specifically in December 19 1990 was born the first Ukraine connection to the World Wide Web. At the time, he headed the sector of the Interdisciplinary Center for Programming Technologies “Technosoft” in Kyiv. Sharing information abroad took only five minutes instead of four hours. The .UA domain didn’t even exist yet.

Technosoft official became an internet provider. Gradually, all major cities in Ukraine got connected. The speed was 2.5 kbps, which felt like pure magic at the time. Users could only read news, use chat rooms and send emails.

After Soviet Union collapsed, the KGB began monitoring all Russian communication lines except internet as they simply didn’t know about it. With that internet become shady thing and was for that for a year, people who knew about internet in silently brought computers to other people and connected to to World Wide Web. But they made a mistake as in Leningrad’s press all newspaper they were producing were with mark that all information was taken from Relcome(one of the networks). Considering all of that KGB started to monitor Russian internet in 1993-1994, but for Ukraine it took another 4 to 5 years to get noticed and so be monitored.
Overview: how it became public
But now let’s summarize and make it into understandable time-diagram

In the spring of 1991, the Kiev-based company "Technosoft" officially received the status of Internet provider. And by the end of 1991, residents of many other cities of Ukraine got access to the Internet.

In autumn 1991, negotiations with the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) on the allocation of its own domain for Ukraine began. And in December 1992 Ukraine finally received its own domain *.ua. Until that time, the only domain of the USSR was *.su, which later became rf (for Russia).

In 1992, the first domestic providers started to appear and actively develop on the Ukrainian market, including Infocom, Tenet, Relcom-Ukraine, Lucky Net, Communication Systems and others. Unlike the USA, where the Internet industry was financed by the state until the mid-90s, in Ukraine Internet deployment took place without state support, which allowed telecom companies to conduct healthy competition. Prior to that, most ISPs operated without a licence, and the authorities had no way to influence this.

In 1994, there were already several dozens of Internet providers in Ukraine, but the scale of Internet penetration was still insignificant. This situation was observed until 1999, when access to the Internet for 10 thousand subscribers in Kiev provided about 50 providers.

In 1994-1996 the first local networks among home users appeared. They united several computers within an apartment block and were used mainly for games and file sharing. A decade later, the most active of them will turn into large Internet providers that will provide access for hundreds, thousands and tens of thousands of subscribers at cosmic speeds for the 90s — tens of megabits per second.
In general, the Ukrainian Internet in the first half of the 90s was used mainly for reading news, chatting and emailing. And already in 1996-1997 the first Ukrainian websites began to appear.

In 1996 the first international fibre-optic communication line (FOCL) was built, which passed through Palermo (Italy), Istanbul (Turkey), Odessa (Ukraine) and had a total length of 3500 km. At the same time, a 975-km long Kiev-Odessa FOCL was opened with branches to Kirovograd, Mykolaiv and Cherkassy.
As for the speed of the Ukrainian Internet, at first it was only 2.5 Kbps. As of 2017, the average Internet speed has increased to 50 Mbps.

In the early 2000s, there were already several hundred Internet providers in Kiev. The real Internet boom in Ukraine began in 2007, when 20% of Ukrainians used the network. In 2012, the number of users was already 50%.

To make the 50% of users they needed more “player” or investors. And with time more and more small players have started to enter the market. After all, another wall was destroyed. Wholesale traffic became much cheaper. Dmitry Verenich, COO of DATA-IX UA, recalls that in 2003 the rates for the wholesale Internet for the providers themselves were high — $2-3 thousand per Mbit / sec.
"But in 2005, Ukrtelecom introduced the Merezheviy transit rate, at which the price of 1Mb of the wholesale Internet was $330, while the market price was $600-1000," he recalls.
Thus, the traffic became much more affordable.

Large associations of home networks, which began to provide services based on the Fiber-to-the-Building technology (FTTB), began to increasingly assert themselves and clearly spoil the nerves of Ukrtelecom and Volia.

Optics allowed beginners to provide much higher speed. And the concept of "limitless" appeared. Download as much as you want at speeds up to 100 Mbps. Although local providers had much lower speed opportunities for traffic entering, nobody talked about it within marketing communications with clients.

With that let’s talk about money for people: between 2004 and 2008, revenues of the first high-speed Internet providers grew rapidly. Prices for this service are becoming available to the mass user and do not exceed UAH 100 per month at the dollar/hryvnia exchange rate of 1:5.

Volia, providing Internet access on the basis of DOCSIS technology, won the leadership in the capital market, and in other cities, Ukrtelecom was strong, which worked under TM OGO! On ADSL. In 2006 Volia began to expand into regions, buying cable providers there. Two big competitors appeared on the market of high-speed Internet.
Little summary

If at the beginning of the '00s, it was just home networks, in the second half of the first decade, FTTB providers learned how to attract investments. They got money for expansion, which scared the market leaders even more.
In 2007, FTH Corvette Telecom bought more than 50% of Kyiv's Internet service provider IPnet. At that time, a very large, as for that time, investment in the Internet was announced — $10 million.
In 2007, Golden Telecom, which later became part of Kyivstar, also announced its FTTB project.
In 2007, Sigma Bleyzer attracted additional investments in its cable business — Volia — amounting to about $200 million from the Providence Equity Partners fund.
In 2008, Horizon Capital bought out a share equal to approximately 30% in operator Datagroup.
It is now about 70%.
So to sum up first mentioning 1980 in USSR, first step in 1991, by 2000 internet had 50% of people in the 2010 all people one way or another had access to world wide web.
The Revolution of Dignity in 2014 brought the internet in Ukraine to a whole new level. Information could now spread quickly. Countless mutual aid groups popped up on Facebook and online forums.

During Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the number of such groups increased dramatically. Thanks to this technology, we can donate to the Armed Forces, follow developments at the front and show the world the truth about what’s happening in our country.
the last Thought
The internet in Ukraine is still developing. Publishing content in Ukrainian, producing local media and dubbing foreign films in our native language will help us build something truly our own.
People who made it public
Yurii Yankovskyi as it founder  and his words how Ukrainian internet was marketed to Ukrainian people:

picture of Yurii Yankovskyi, a man setting on the beach around him are Reed (lat. Scírpus) he wears a grey sweater, jeanes and pair of white boots. Yurii looks at the camera and smiles.  https://www.radiosvoboda.org/a/24908485.html
From his interview about how he marketed the internet

“I had a piece of paper with a picture of the globe, wires surrounding it, and explaining what the Internet was and how it worked. We didn't advertise in the press or on television. We went to conferences and chose companies that would be interested. Well, let's say, Khimvolokno or a machine-building plant. We explained to them: this is the post office, and here you can read the news, you can enter information about yourself. When people joined, they willingly started paying money. By the end of 1991, we had a hundred clients.”
The first home networks appeared in 1994-1996. They were created by enthusiasts to communicate or for computer games to play on the net.
photo of Yuriy Lazebnikov. A man looks at the camera, he looks tired, has a bread, wears blue shirt. https://techiia.com/ru/team/yuriy-lazebnikov

"Esports began to develop rapidly when it became possible to connect computers in a single network. Local networks seemed to be created for games — they eliminate the distance between players, create a sense of presence at the moment, make it possible to track the results in real time," says Yuriy Lazebnikov. He knows what he's talking about because the TECHIA holding includes the esports company WePlay! Esports.

Such networks had no access to the outside world. They combined several computers in one house or dormitory. At the end of the last century, the number of Internet subscribers even in Kyiv did not exceed 10 thousand, and they were served by almost 50 providers.
Over time, small networks were united and turned into providers with an extensive home infrastructure.

"The airies" — cables flipped from one roof to another, switches in attics or in cellars — that' s what the first home networks looked like in the late '90s. Personal contacts with ZhEK representatives were very expensive, as all this "equipment" was placed illegally, and their safety was ensured by usual "hangar" locks. At the same time, such "home networks" allowed the first fans of computer games to taste the victory over the real rivals, while the game producers were improving all the graphics and content.

Here we must say that the provisioning business in Ukraine is not licensed now and was not licensed then. This reduced the market entry threshold for providers of "home networks" and created a competitive environment. And it means that anyone and everyone could compete for a place in the sun.
photo of Alexander Harutyunyan, amn sitting on a chair, lowkey looking crazy, is in balck hoodie, has goatee like beard and has not too much hair.

"One house — one network, shared Internet, network chat, dc++ file-sharing client. Ah, what a time it was. Then the houses began to merge into large networks. Not everyone could afford the optics, so it makes sense that we had to unite," recalls Alexander Harutyunyan, CEO of Internet provider Pavutina.

photo of Sergey Boyko, standing like his thinking about stealing the moon but i think it will be more practical - about money, wears grey costumes with baby blue striped shirt. he has a beard, on background is stairs, on the left bicycle and behind him a little cafe.
Sergey Boyko, former president of Volia, recalls how more or less large organized Internet business started in Kyiv in the '00s.

"In 2000-2001, the first fiber-optic ring was built, covering all districts of Kyiv," he says. This element of infrastructure was very important, as local home Internet networks could not provide high-speed access to either the Ukrainian segment of the Internet or the international one.
In other words, an urban transport network for traffic was built.

"In 2002, based on the acquired enterprises, the company Volia was established, and in 2003 for the first time, a high-speed (128/256 Kbps) access was provided throughout the right bank of Kyiv," he specifies.
Oleg Krot wears black shirt, goatee beard and smiles to the camera

"The inexpensive access to high-speed Internet and the economic upturn in 2005-2008 caused a real boom in interest in eSports. Many could already afford computers for video games or went to computer clubs, the popularity of which then grew dramatically. People came to Internet cafes not only to play but also to communicate with each other, whole clans of players were created. It was in 2006, my partner Oleg Krot and I created our first club, which then grew into a business based on eSports," says Yuriy Lazebnikov.
All resources
Internet when I was born: my experience
Small intro to when I was born.

Let’s start about my life with internet. I was born in small village in 2006. In Kyiv, Kharkiv, Donetsk(I will use Ukrainian pronunciation instead of Russian with Kiev and Kharkov) and some other major cities already have internet and started creating sites, all other only heard of internet or World Wide Web. But let’s talk about some statistic: according to the Bigmir)net portal, the unique audience of the Ukrainian Internet reached 2.418 million users in December 2005.
What it was like (official)

At the beginning of December 2006, the Ukrainian Internet audience reached 3.437 million users. This means the number of Internet users in Ukraine has grown by more than a million, and in relative terms, by almost 50%.

The number of domains (second- and third-level) registered in the .UA territorial domain at the beginning of the year was 175,441. By the beginning of December 2006, this number had reached 234,149. The number of domains on the Ukrainian Internet has grown by almost 60,000, or a third compared to the previous year.
This trend is encouraging: the number of Internet users in Ukraine is growing, and the number of websites is increasing—the Ukrainian Internet is growing, and at a fairly rapid pace.

With that more and more people creating web sites people start use internet with more serious thoughts in mind. Web sites about politics, news, money and just something more than little games started to take other internet.

And here are some sites that took some nomination on different themes. In December, InternetUA magazine named the leaders of the Ukrainian internet. The publication's journalists and experts selected the most dynamic portals and websites, providers' technological achievements, and those who attracted widespread interest with their innovative solutions and active development.
The following received awards
"Portal of the Year" — Online.ua,
"Email Service of the Year" — Ukr.net,
"Search Engine of the Year" — Google.com.ua,
"Update of the Year" — Meta.ua,
"News Resource of the Year" — Korrespondent.net,
"Web Service of the Year" — MP3.ua,
"Startup of the Year" — Gismeteo.ua,
"Community of the Year" — Dynamo.kiev.ua,
"Web Store of the Year" — Petrovka.ua,
"Trading System of the Year" — Price.ua,
"Hosting Provider of the Year" — Volia Data Center,
"Mobile Internet Provider of the Year" — UMC,
and "High-Speed ​​Provider of the Year" — Volia.
Not official stories from people

But now let’s talk about what people felt about internet and it was mixed at best negative at worst. As we saw from previous text there were more and more people using it, feeling it was helpful or just interesting, but there also people who didn’t like it. For example I found pretty stupid message but worth mentioning to show what some people(especially people 30 to 40 plus) thought about that. Please don’t take it seriously!
“Your attitude towards the Internet
Three words that describe the Internet for me (expletives)

When I'm online, I sweat. 3. Most websites on the Internet are crap. 4. Time flies online. 5. The Internet helps me experience the highest pleasure. 6. Most people on the Internet are cool intellectuals. 7. After logging off the Internet, I feel like an idiot. 8. I'm attracted to porn sites and perversions on the Internet. 9. My favorite thing about the Internet is pop-up ads. 10. Other people on the Internet seem smarter than me. 11. When I'm online, I have a hard time getting to the bathroom. 12. All the information on the Internet is valuable and useful. 13. Dead links don't irritate me. 14. When I click links, I feel a slight tingling in my left heel. 15. The Internet doesn't interfere with my life. 16. I visit websites to get sexual satisfaction. 17. The Internet is useless unless you use it. 18. I think the Internet is for sick people. Result: 17 "yes" answers - you are a full user. Up to 9 "yes" answers - you are a half-user. From 0 to 9 "yes" answers - you are a normal person.”
Or

“People treated it like video games—you know, just a niche geek thing that only nerds liked. I never thought it was just a fad, but I also didn't predict it would become such a universal thing that everyone would use, rather than being relegated to the realm of nerds.”
Also I think i need to explain user thing, in Ukrainian user word is  sounds pretty similar to looser. So when this person calls “you a full user” it actually means “you a full looser”.
But there were and more enthusiastic thoughts about internet

“I think it was pretty obvious that the internet would change our lives. We immediately realized how useful it would be for exchanging information and ideas, as well as for business and marketing. I was a little surprised by how it seemed to speed up the pace of life and everything else. Everything happens instantly. Everything seems urgent.”

“It was incredibly cool to be able to email someone, go to the library and read books and articles, chat with people from all over the world, and so on. At first, when I clicked on links from a system request, it also felt a bit like a club for the elite, where you could access all sorts of information and experiences that most people couldn't get simply because they didn't understand how the internet "worked.””

With all of that in mind I will conclude that it was varied from person to person. If someone liked working with computers and was in big city where internet was a thing than yes, most likely they liked internet all ideas it had. If you were in small town where internet was bourgeoisie thing to enjoy, then of course you will at least king of hate it. For example my father was in love with internet, he quickly realized how many benefits it could give and started using it, with that it was positive thinking. For my grandfather it was games and thing that will not last long, so more negative thoughts.
So let’s sum up all of that with fine words

In 2006, the number of Internet users exceeded 4 million. Internet providers began to provide access to the network using a "dedicated line", the speed increased, and the network became more similar to the web environment in which we live today. But it is worth noting an important feature: before social networks and instant messengers penetrated our everyday lives, using the Internet was much "more interesting", and it was done more thoughtfully.

There was an environment for each purpose. Those who wanted to share their thoughts registered on the LiveJournal platform, where a sense of security and freedom reigned, and quarrels in the comments were rather the exception to the rule. Those who craved communication sat in the legendary online chats, where they had to invent a nickname for themselves and try to find a common language in complete anonymity. For more in-depth communication on interests — from computers to music — there were forums where you had to monitor your statements and reputation, troublemakers were disliked and immediately "banned." Each site performed a certain function, while now we are all useless travelers on several crowded platforms of Facebook, YouTube, Twitter, and Instagram: we try to attract attention and solve all our problems at once, but we are heard less and less.
What it brought to people

With internet people were able to speak with their family in another city/country, be able to read more books, read more news, listen to music and more and more. But with that also worth mentioning impact of the internet on economy and other part of society.
Politics

In politics internet slowly but surely took place to influence elections. For example, it may be speculation as with all of the politics, but it is not a surprise that Zelenski won elections partial because of the fame in the internet. People want normal human to represent them and why not take person who played as president and in the movie did a pretty dissent job?
Here is what I am talking about, also what newspapers say:(take with a lot of grain of scepticism as a lot of the article may be also payed to talk about that so I will say it’s truth for 30%)

“Volodymyr Zelensky’s victory confirms that mastering social media and its features is one of the decisive factors for political success today. In contrast to Facebook and VK, it was on Instagram that we recorded the highest percentage of positive and neutral posts. One of the reasons is that such content is generated by numerous Ze accounts and fan pages. While other parties tried to catch up on what was lost on this social network with the start of the parliamentary election campaign, Servant of the People (SN) was far ahead.

Having completely mastered the social network with a reputation for being a platform about “frivolous things”, this party packaged politics into Instagram formats at the speed of light. Zelensky and his team’s hashtags were even used in posts about “European Solidarity”, “Fatherland” and “Voice”.

Vakarchuk's political force came late to the scene, but the fact that it made it to parliament underscores our thesis: large-scale involvement in social media can be an almost decisive part of an electoral strategy. Today, social media is also a political tool. Those who underestimated this weapon were left behind.”
Economic
If we are talking about how internet influenced out economy we should talk about first job to come in mind is IT, programmers and all sub categories of that profession named in Ukraine as IT.

IT, according to the results of 2022, the industry provided revenues to the Ukrainian economy in the amount of $ 7.35 billion or 4.5% of GDP (Fig. 2). The number of companies and enterprises in Ukraine that provide digital services is about 8.2 thousand, with 5 thousand of them looking for new employees, and about 1.5 thousand companies are technology startups. According to DOU, as of the end of February 2023, 271,699 individual entrepreneurs worked in the IT sector, which is 13.6% of all active individual entrepreneurs for that period (although some individual entrepreneurs may actually be employees, this is a significant share).
On the picture
on the top of the image: dynamics of IT export.

Than goes data vis with such information: in 2018 - 3204 billion dollars, in 2019 - 4173 billion dollars, in 2020 - 5 026 billion dollars, in 2021 - 6943 billion dollars, in 2022 - 7349 billion dollars.
In the end, at the bottom of picture they clarify that numbers wich goes up with the years pass by is export, billion dollars.

If we are talking globally then with help of the Internet, there are ATM's which can be used with online cards, Internet banking,  the ability to pay by phone, transfer money to another person just with click on the telephone and much more.
In fact, digital technologies and the World Wide Web have turned the entire financial industry upside down and created a whole range of new services and equipment that were impossible before.

With that also same happened to books and other instruction for learning something new, as only in the age of digital transformation has it become possible to sell the same product multiple times.You can buy an e-book, a knitting pattern, a photo, a song or a film - and the original will remain in the hands of the owner.
Digital maps instead of paper maps

People who live in big cities or love to travel cannot imagine their life without a map on their smartphone. After all, it not only shows routes, your location and the distance to your desired destination. Here you can see photos of any point on the planet that people have already visited, read reviews about establishments, find out about traffic jams, walk the streets of unknown cities.
Previously, we would ask passers-by how to get to this or that place, but now we can make a detailed itinerary, just holding the phone in our hands.
Remote work or studying online

This is one of the most favorite innovations of office workers and students, thanks to which you can work/study lying down, go to zoom meetings without pants and not spend 2-3 hours a day on the road.

This way of working leads to other changes - a decrease in the number of people in transport and the abandonment of offices, schools and universities. Already now, IT companies are reducing office space, because after the coronavirus epidemic, both employees and management liked working from home. Schools and Universities are encourage and often do same things, cause with that theu can take more students and it means more money.
However, not everyone is happy with such innovations. If you have three children, a dog, a parrot and two cats, then working or studying in different place is almost a vacation!
All of these are always affecting economics in one way or another.

And with that I will say that internet and it’s possibilities affect not only decisions of ordinary people but the ones who have authority as these people decided to do app(Дія or Dya is app for storing your passport and a lot more documents) which will stored all your necessary and a little bit of sensitive information but let’s talk about this in details.

The problems of informatization of state authorities, state organizations and enterprises are set out in the Decree of the President of Ukraine No. 663 of June 17, 1997 and the National Program of Informatization of Ukraine.

Despite all the problems, flaws, and existing limitations, the Internet is increasingly being used in government institutions. There are information system projects where technical solutions are made based on the capabilities and technologies of the Internet (in these systems, information does not fall into the category of restricted access).

The aforementioned Presidential Decree and the National Informatization Program provide for a number of tasks related to the creation of corporate networks of research institutions, ministries, and departments. Given the limited funding, the Internet may be the basic option for some projects.

The Diya application , introduced in Ukraine in 2020, allows you to store your driver's license, domestic and international passports and other documents in your smartphone , as well as transfer their copies when receiving banking or postal services, checking into a hotel and in other life situations. Also, through Diya (application and/or portal), you can receive such services as eMalyatko (a comprehensive service at the birth of a child), register a business and individual entrepreneur online, pay taxes and file declarations, sign any documents, change the place of registration, etc.

During 2019, mobile operators significantly expanded the coverage of the territory of Ukraine with 4G networks, which allowed to increase to 78% the share of the population that can receive mobile broadband Internet access services. At the same time, the expansion of the coverage area of ​​4G networks and the widespread distribution of consumer end devices running operating systems (smartphones, tablets) led to an increase in the volume of mobile Internet traffic consumption . In addition, the number of consumers of machine-to-machine ( M2M ) and Internet of Things ( IoT ) services continues to gradually increase. Thus, the provision of data transmission services, including Internet access, is becoming an increasingly key revenue item for mobile operators every year  .

With that expansion if the internet on December 17, 2024, the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine adopted in the first reading draft law No. 12094 on regulation in the field of electronic communications, which should ensure access to high-speed Internet in rural areas and on roads. The document provides for the establishment of standards for Internet connection speed outside large settlements. An important component of the draft law is the introduction of a communication quality control system  .
How it developed to our times
Next will do more detailed official explanation about all the years till now(2026), I will write short answer in italics near the year.
1990s : first internet in (post)Soviet space.

The first Internet users appeared in Ukraine during Soviet times. In 1990, the first three network access nodes were created (via Moscow to Finland) and support for the Ukrainian part of the Internet domain space — the ".ua" domain — began  . At first, it was supported informally, and on December 1, 1992, it was officially delegated to Ukraine. From that time on, the history of the Ukrainian segment of the Internet began .

By the end of 1991, the provider "Technosoft" served subscribers in all major cities of Ukraine. The speed was 2.5 kilobits per second. In February 1993, Uarnet, together with the Swedish Space Corporation, built the first satellite communication channel in Ukraine (via the Tele-X satellite ), which became the first direct connection in Ukraine to the global Internet .
In December 1999, Aiken Studio was created  - one of the first "smithies" of Ukrainian websites .
2000 IAU
In November 2000, the Internet Association of Ukraine (IAU) was established.
2006 the rise of the Internet in Ukraine
Diagram of changes in the number of Internet users in Ukraine, 2000-2010
In January 2006, the size of the Ukrainian Internet audience was 4,207,391 unique users according to the website Sputnikmedia.net (out of reach) .

The website internetworldstats.com (out of reach) gives a figure of 5,278,100 users as of March 2005, which is 11.5% of the population of Ukraine. The increase in the Internet audience in Ukraine in 2000–2007 was 2539.1%. Since in 2000 there were 200,000 users and this was approximately 0.4% of the total population of Ukraine.
The website " Suspilne "  estimated the number of Internet users at over 4 million in 2006.
2007 the first torrent tracker

According to the press service of the Department of Communications and Informatization of the Ministry of Transport and Communications, as of January 1, 2007, there were about 9 million users in Ukraine. This is approximately 18.75% of the population of Ukraine.
According to TNS Ukraine, about 7 million users access the Internet at least once a week.
The first free Ukrainian torrent tracker  , Hurtom, has been launched .
2008 10 million people used internet
May
The number of Ukrainian Internet users in May 2008 increased by 0.77% compared to April and amounted to 8,471,954 unique users per month  .

In terms of geographical distribution, the Kyiv region held the lead . Its share was 58.82% of the total number of visitors. This was followed by Odessa , Dnipro , Donetsk , Kharkiv , Lviv , Zaporizhia , and Crimea . Their total share was 30.89%. The least activity of Internet users was noted in Chernivtsi and Zhytomyr regions  - only 0.17% each. Other regions accounted for 9.95% of users.
According to the then data of TNS Ukraine and the research company InMind , the number of users of the Ukrainian Internet was 10 million subscribers.
October

The size of the Ukrainian audience of Internet users (unique users who made more than one page view in October 2008 and users who viewed pages in October and September) amounted to 10,164,517 people as of the end of October, which is 6% more than in
September  .
In percentage terms: Kyiv was ahead by a significant margin — 58.96% of all Internet users in Ukraine.

Next were Odesa (6.43%), Dnipro (5.62%), Donetsk (5.41%), Kharkiv (4.44%), Lviv (3.58%), Zaporizhia (2.47%), Crimea (2.70%). The total share of these regions in percentage terms is 30.65%. Other regions of Ukraine accounted for less than 10% of Internet users.
The lowest rates are: Lutsk (0.17%), Zhytomyr (0.21%), and Chernivtsi (0.22%).
December

41% of Ukrainians living in cities with a population of over 50,000 used the Internet . The number of Internet users in cities was 6.638 million, with the largest age group being 30-39-year-old Ukrainians (22.8%). The typically large “youth” group (14-19 years old) used network services somewhat less — 19.3%. There were even fewer Internet users aged 40-49 — 15%. Overall, 43.2% of the population in the 25-40 age group used the Internet.
87% of the Internet audience visited the World Wide Web at least once a week, while 44% visited once or more times a day.
The incomes of 18% of Internet users were considered high and 66% were considered average.
2009  30% of Ukraine used internet daily.
March

The Ukrainian audience of Internet users (users who made more than one page view in March 2009, and users who viewed pages in March and in the previous month) in March 2009 amounted to 11.96 million people, which is 9.4% more than in February 2009.

In the regional distribution of users, Kyiv was the leader by a significant margin, accounting for 60.14% of all Internet users in Ukraine in March. It was followed by Odesa (5.14%), Kharkiv (5.39%), Dnipro (4.92%), Donetsk (4.82%), Lviv (2.83%), Crimea (2.80%), Zaporizhia (1.80%). The total share of these regions was 24.9%.
The remaining regions of Ukraine accounted for 13.26% of users.
Outsiders in terms of Internet penetration in March 2009 were Lutsk (0.18%), Zhytomyr (0.21%), Chernivtsi (0.32%), and Transcarpathia (0.31%)  .
November

According to the results of the fall of 2009, the Internet audience in Ukraine amounted to 8.25 million unique users, which is 1.613 million more than the similar indicator in the fall of 2008. This was evidenced by the results of a study conducted by GfK Ukraine on behalf of the Ukrainian Association of Internet Advertising (UAIA).

The highest growth rate of Internet visitors was observed in cities with a population of 50-100 thousand inhabitants - the growth for the year was 14%. Also in 2009, the Internet was used much more actively - daily coverage in that year was 31% of Ukrainian residents in cities with a population of 50 thousand or more (including million-plus cities) compared to 18% in 2008 .
2010 12,900,000 Internet users in Ukraine

In April 2010, a study by Gemius Ukraine showed 8.669 million regular users aged 16 and older (i.e. an annual growth of 1.438 million compared to April 2009). At the same time, InMind data was published at the request of InAU : 12 million or Internet access coverage at 32%.
In June, Bigmir had 18,581,501 unique users  .
As of October 6, 2010, there were 12,900,000 Internet users in Ukraine, according to InMind .

33% of all residents of Ukraine visited the Internet at least once a month. Over eight million Internet users lived in large cities and towns with a population of more than 50 thousand, 4.6 million users lived in smaller settlements.
Of all Ukrainian Internet users aged 15 and over, 8.7 million used the Internet every day or almost every day.
The highest level of Internet penetration was among young age groups: 61% of the population aged 15 to 29 years used the world wide web, 39% of people aged 30 to 44 years  .
2011 90% of Kyiv had access to internet

According to the results of a study conducted by the International Center for Policy Studies ( ICPS ), published in September 2011 in the WEEKLYUA magazine, a comparative situation with access to the Internet by residents of different regions of Ukraine was published . The least opportunities for access were for residents of the Rivne region, where the figure barely reached 2%, which in international terms corresponded to the level of Afghanistan . The best opportunities for choosing Internet access services were for residents of Kyiv, where about 90% of Kyiv residents were users of the World Wide Web, which in international terms corresponded to the level of the Netherlands .

“In some metropolitan high-rise buildings, 14 cable operators are ready to provide their services to residents at once, while in many small towns and villages, people are still forced to occupy a telephone line to access the network.”— International Center for Advanced Studies, WEEKLYUA magazine
2012 48% of Ukrainians had access to internet
According to the analysis of the Internet audience in Ukraine , in March 2012, 48% of Ukrainian residents aged 15 and older had access to the Internet. The following people used the Internet:
42% (16.9 million) once a month or more
39% (15.7 million) once a week or more
31% (12.4 million) daily, or almost daily.

The number of Internet subscribers in Ukraine in the second quarter of 2012 compared to the second quarter of 2011 increased by 30% and amounted to 4.64 million people , moreover, more than 4.2 million of them were home subscribers.
2013 50% of Ukraine used and had internet

According to the Kyiv International Institute of Sociology (KIIS) , in September 2013, 49.8% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. Thus, the growth in the number of users continued at an even faster pace than expected. The growth rate during February 2012 - October 2013 was 16%, which is slightly lower than the largest jump of 34% in the period from March 2011 to February 2012  .
Diagram of changes in the number of Internet users in Ukraine 2000-2013 (source: ITU)
A less optimistic figure of 41.8% was published  by the International Telecommunication Union .
2014 More Ukrainian bloggers

In recent years, especially after 2014, a large number of Ukrainian-language YouTube channels for children have appeared  . The number of Ukrainian-language bloggers on YouTube has been steadily increasing .
2015 “Telecom Ukraine 2015”- Ukrainian conference

In September 2015, the largest event of the year for the telecom industry took place in the city of Odessa - the All-Ukrainian Conference "Telecom Ukraine 2015", which brought together over 300 participants - heads of enterprises in the communications, informatization and telecommunications sectors, state representatives, public figures, world manufacturers of modern telecommunications equipment and other delegates interested in the development of the domestic industry.

The initiators and organizers of the Conference were InAU, State Enterprise "UA-IX", Telecom Summit and Telecom Fiesta. Having joined forces to prepare such a popular event, the organizers set themselves the goal of uniting the largest possible number of participants not only from the telecommunications industry of Ukraine, but also representatives of cable TV and the media industry, in order to involve all components of the domestic informatization industry in communication and interaction with each other and with the state. The following specialized associations were also invited to cooperate and took an active part in the preparation of the program: APITU , APPK, TelPU, VAOKKT and Ukrtelemerezha  .

The number of Internet users in 2015 continued to grow faster than expected. The growth rate during February 2015–February 2016 was 8%. Longer-term comparisons have not been possible since 2014, as the situation in the country changed dramatically due to the aggression by the Russian Federation . Nevertheless, despite all the difficulties, Internet use has been steadily growing—in the period from February 2014 to February 2016 alone, the number of Internet users increased by 7 percentage points, and the growth rate was 13%  .
2016 91% of teenagers used internet

According to data from the KIIS (published on their official website on May 4, 2016), which presented the results of a survey conducted during February 2016, at the beginning of the year 61.6% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. The share of Internet users among people aged 18-39 in Ukraine reached 91%. Older age and living in rural areas significantly reduced the likelihood of using the Internet. The survey was conducted in all regions of Ukraine except for the occupied Crimea and the temporarily uncontrolled territories of Donbass  .
The National Bank's 2015 Annual Report was first distributed exclusively through online channels in two versions — a full-color version for viewing and an ECO version for printing  .
2017 more than 60% of adults used internet

According to the Internet Association of Ukraine (distributed on their official website in April 2017), which presented the results of a survey conducted during February 2017, at the beginning of the year 64.7% of the adult population of Ukraine used the Internet. The share of Internet visitors among people aged 15-29 in Ukraine reached 97%. Older age and living in rural areas significantly reduced the likelihood of using the Internet. The survey was conducted in all regions of Ukraine except for the occupied Crimea and the temporarily uncontrolled territories of Donbas. The number of Internet users continued to grow. Most often, they accessed the Internet using a stationary PC  - 51%, mobile phones  - 50%, laptops  - 42%, tablet computers  - 21%, stationary PCs at work - 8%, laptops at work - 5%. About 21.6 million users used the Internet in Ukraine. Women made up 51% of all users, men 49%. Villagers made up 27% of the total, while among all village residents, 53% of them used the Internet  .
2018 UAH 62 billion(in Ukraine currency) to communications market

As of the end of 2018, thousands of business entities were operating in the telecommunications market, most of them being individual entrepreneurs. The main indicators of the industry for 2018 were as follows: the total annual revenue of the communications market amounted to UAH(in Ukraine currency) 62 billion, which is 10.2% more compared to 2017. Almost half of this - UAH 35 billion - was the revenue of mobile operators. The growth rate for the reporting year 2018 was 11% compared to 2017. In 2018, mobile operators invested a total of about UAH 10 billion in the industry (excluding the cost of 4G licenses)  . The most visited sites at the end of the year were: google, youtube.com, facebook.com, ukr.net, vkontakte (vk.com), olx.ua, privatbank.ua, yandex, odnoklassniki (ok.ru), sinoptik.ua, instagram.com, prom.ua.
2019 85% of teenagers used internet daily
According to Gemius, as of June 2019, there were 24.8 million Internet users in Ukraine.
According to the annual Kantar Ukraine survey (out of reach now) 2019, 74% of the Ukrainian population (aged 12-70) used the Internet. 85 percent of them used it every day.
19 million Ukrainians used their own smartphones to access the Internet.
In 2019, Ukraine ranked only 82nd, despite the fact that in 2018, it was in 58th place in the ranking of countries with high-speed Internet .

At the same time, as of September 2019, Ukraine ranked fourth among all EU -39 countries in terms of the number of FTTH/B-nodes (Fiber To The Building) and FTTH (Fiber To The Home) — 11.24 million units. Only Spain, France, and Russia had more. In total, there were about 400 million FTTH/B-nodes in the EU-39, half of which were in the TOP-5 countries .

Despite the fact that in 2019 Ukrainians began to trust the media much less (trust in all traditional media fell by 11% compared to 2018), and trust in particular fluctuates between 19% in the national press, 22% in local radio, and 49% in national TV channels — according to USAID-Internews, 51% of respondents trusted national online media .
2020 from 19 million to 26 million because of coronavirus

During the year of quarantine related to the COVID-19 coronavirus pandemic, from the beginning of 2020 to the beginning of 2021, that is, in 2020, the Ukrainian social media audience increased by 7 million people, according to a report by GlobalLogic , citing the results of a study conducted based on open data.

“At the beginning of 2020, there were 19 million [social media] users in Ukraine, in 2021 the figure reached 26 million. At the same time, the penetration of social media has increased by half: currently 60% of the country's population is registered with them, while in January 2020 it was just over 40%,” this message notes .

In general, digital culture is actively developing in Ukraine, because from October 2020 to January 2021 alone, the number of 5G smartphones in Ukraine increased from 31 thousand to 194 thousand units  . At the same time, the number of 4G smartphones has increased from 30 million to 39.9 million units during the same time.
2021 30 millions users
As of early 2021, the number of Ukrainian Internet users was almost 30 million, or about 67% of the country's population  .

In 2021, the Internet overtook television as a source of information in Ukraine for the first time. According to a survey conducted by Research&Branding Group, 51% of Ukrainians surveyed prefer the Internet as a source of information, while 44% prefer television, and 2% prefer radio and print media.

Also, in 2021, Ukrainians began to encounter scammers on the Internet twice as often. Cybercriminals most often attack users through popular instant messengers, and email is almost not in demand during online shopping.

Over the past six months, 45% of Ukrainians surveyed have encountered online fraud attempts. Last year, this figure was half as low — 22%. Most often, since the beginning of 2021, residents of large cities have been attacked — ⅔ of cases . Also, according to Verizon, in 2021, 36% of cybersecurity breaches were related to phishing attacks, during which attackers disguised themselves as employees of institutions that inspire trust among users and demand confidential information. The main topic used by scammers in 2021 was the coronavirus pandemic . Attackers changed their phishing campaigns depending on the news.
2022 62th place in Ookla speed test

Ukraine has risen 15 places in the global mobile internet speed ranking, according to the Ookla Speedtest Global Index, from 77th place in January 2021 to 62nd place in January 2022. According to Ookla research, the average download speed on the mobile network increased by almost 10 Mbps — from 19.66 Mbps at the beginning of 2021 to 29.06 Mbps in 2022 .
2023 - 2024 speed of 80.2 Mbps

According to the rating of the popular resource Speedtest , which measures the speed of online connections, it became known that in 2024 Ukraine occupies one of the last places in the world in terms of mobile Internet performance. According to data for June 2024, the country occupies 93rd place out of 108 measured, with an average speed of 21.24 Mbps. This is 2.5 times lower than the world average, which is marked by the insufficient efficiency of the Internet infrastructure on mobile networks in Ukraine. The situation with fixed Internet in Ukraine is somewhat better: Ukraine occupies 70th place among 159 countries with a speed of 80.2 Mbps .
So for summary till 2020 it was rising after that it started to encounter a lot of problems, don’t get me started to talk about them(war)

Differencies from other countries and a little bit more about money
By mid-2012, Ukraine was among the top ten countries in Europe in terms of the number of Internet users   — up to 15 million Ukrainians had access to the World Wide Web.

In Ukraine, about a third (33%) of the population were Internet users  , and, for example, according to internetworldstats.com  , in the USA 69.6% of the population uses the Internet, in Germany 61.3%, in the UK 50.3%, in France 50.3%, in Poland (62%) and in Russia (44%). In 2012, 50.9% of the population of the European Union used the World Wide Web.
In terms of the cost of home ( fixed ) Internet, Ukraine ranked " third from the bottom "  among 160 countries according to the International Telecommunication Union rating .
In 2012, the average monthly cost of Internet access in Ukraine was just over 75 hryvnias .

As of the end of 2020, compared to other countries, Internet access in Ukraine was quite inexpensive. The cheapest package cost $ 3.6 per month, and the most expensive — $13.5. The average cost of network access was $6.5 (about 180 UAH). For comparison, a package in the PRC and Georgia cost a little more than $12 per month. In Lithuania, the cheapest package cost $3.5 per month (cheaper than in Ukraine), the most expensive — $28.2. The average monthly cost is $12.9. On average, Internet access in France and Italy cost $31.7 per month. In the UK, such a service is even more expensive. The cheapest package cost $23, the most expensive — $68. The average cost per month was $35.7  .

Situation now and prediction
Overall and short predictions
Predictions more like speculations

To make long story short future promises that internet will have more and more influence on out lives than now. More VR features, more helpful AI assistance, more work and jobs will be reconstructed to work on internet more than already.

No matter how advanced technologies become, the principle of their personalized work will always be based on the analysis of input information. The innovations of the future will be able to process incoming data streams even better and issue the most appropriate decisions, as a result of which human standards of living will increase significantly. After all, we live better than any kings of the Middle Ages - it is quite possible that in a few decades any beggar or poor person will have opportunities that presidents and multimillionaires do not have today.
Overall situation

In general, the number of Ukrainians who regularly use the “network of networks” has increased significantly over the years 2012-2023: in 2012, the Internet penetration rate in Ukraine was 33.7%, and in 2023 – 80%. Such a jump was made possible, in particular, due to the developed Internet infrastructure, a wide 4G network, relatively low prices for provider services, and the absence of strict control by the state.

However, although Internet penetration rates continue to grow, certain obstacles to Internet access remain relevant, such as damage to infrastructure due to Russian shelling, the transfer of temporarily occupied territories to Russian-controlled Internet access networks, etc.

The Internet infrastructure in Ukraine is diverse and open. The connection of backbone data transmission networks used to connect several regions to each other to the global “network of networks” is decentralized. Most Ukrainian ISPs manage their own communication networks. To enable ISPs to exchange traffic from their networks and connect to the global Internet, there are at least 23 Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) in Ukraine, 12 of which were operational as of March 2024. Internet Exchange Points provide faster data exchange, increased reliability, and fewer service outages for ISPs by providing direct connections between ISPs, thereby improving the overall quality and availability of the Internet.

In recent years, Ukraine has consistently ranked high (top 10) in international rankings of the reliability of the national Internet segment for the fourth version of the Internet Protocol (IPv4) and the sixth version of the Internet Protocol (IPv6) – this is one of the indicators of a well-developed Internet infrastructure in Ukraine.

Internet technologies can be implemented in various devices, be it gadgets like smartphones, VR glasses or even implants implanted in the brain. But they will have one thing in common: the ability to predict events and build the optimal course of action for a person. Taxi services already receive information from satellites and lead the driver along the least busy route. It is easy to imagine that future predictors will not only display the optimal route for the user, but also give advice on choosing a university or company for work, predict his health and protect against certain diseases.
Mixed feelings today

People have mixed feelings for VR and internet some people think taht it will get worst some think that situation will get better but something that we can’t change and all agree on is that internet one way or another will always influence peoples.
For example of Internet's influence on people

Recently, the South Korean supermarket Tesco conducted an experiment, the results of which the authors considered positive. The company opened a virtual store right in the subway, using the columns and walls of the station as a showcase. While passengers were waiting for the train, they could study the product catalog by simply holding their phone up to the image of the products. Sales increased by 130%, and although the marketing may have seemed too intrusive, it also saved people time waiting for transport.

I will say I have mixed feelings because it’s kind of harsh marketing, because people may be tired so they were vulnerable and longing for things that will give them short term of dopamine and so happiness in life, so market give them that opportunity to fill rush while buying things. And that contains a problem how much these cloth pieces were returned? How much people just decided to throw away after it was delivered to them? If even not thrown or returned, does it really contained good intentions for people or they wanted to sell more than they can ever sell?From other side, such sales means money not only for rich people and companies but also more money for ordinary workers and better impact on economic.
We already talked about VR sales and internet sales but another theme that gets mixed feelings is digital privacy.

Have you seen gadgets like smart glasses in many movies about the future that scan every passerby, providing detailed information about them? Put two and two together: the rapid development of IT technologies and the desire of regulators to collect maximum information about users, which is something that both government agencies and private corporations agree on. Today, anonymity is a whim, and tomorrow it may become a luxury.

A virtual assistant or an advanced search engine will know more about you than you do yourself. Your work skills, hobbies, favorite places - all this is information that can be collected. By the way, some online banks can structure expenses on the account, distributing them by type of expenses. It is enough to integrate data into the same assistant, which will know where, how and how much money you spend. What's next? A smart feed of commercial offers - this is what can await a person in the future. This is the same personification. Think about how social networks and advertising services worked 10-15 years ago and how they work now: time will pass, and the Internet will become an ideal place to spend time for everyone, as the entire web environment will adapt to the user's interests. Only there will be no place for privacy there. And it’s a huge problem as some privacy should be! People don’t want other people to know their sensitive information that in the end can harm them.

But with that again isn’t kind of great for some disabled people to get more help? For people who can’t see, AI assistant that will guide them everywhere and at anytime. Same can be applied to disabled people, elderly people and some people who has some other problems.
Speculations about the future
But let’s talk about future of the internet globally and hope that Ukraine will get it not after another 10 to 20+ years.
The Metaverse and Immersive Experiences

In the future internet, digital (online) activities will merge with virtual and physical worlds to create realistic, immersive experiences. Brands, schools, employers, and individuals will all operate their own virtual worlds where people can gather to learn, game, work, or socialize.

The metaverse — a persistent, shared digital environment where we can move from activity to activity with the same avatar, digital assets, and preferences — brings together technologies like augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), and digital twins to create a more immersive, realistic and engaging internet.
The Rise of Decentralization

In the decentralized internet, power and control are distributed rather than centralized. In the future, the internet will increasingly be powered by decentralized technologies like blockchain and peer-to-peer networks rather than being controlled by a few government entities or large corporations. This would allow for greater privacy and security and will cut down on censorship. A decentralized internet will also enable new types of applications and services that are not possible on the current centralized internet.
Three concepts that play a central role in web3 are
Blockchain: A method of storing information built around distributed computing, encryption, and transparency.

Advanced Hardware: Hardware innovations like goggles, glasses, and headsets will help speed up the next iteration of the future internet.Big tech companies are not only creating cutting-edge VR headsets for when we want a fully immersive internet experience, but they're also working on devices like smart glasses that we can comfortably wear while walking around and interacting in the real world.

I recently tried out a prototype of smart glasses made by Viture. These glasses look just like regular sunglasses, but they display a 120-inch virtual screen in front of your face. With these glasses, I could block out the real world to play a game or watch a video, or look through them just like normal glasses.

Smart glasses of the future will bring augmented reality to life by displaying digital images and information in front of our eyes as we move through our days. This will be one way we’ll incorporate AR into the way we work, play, and live.
It could be possible that with this new wearable technology, we may reach a point where we no longer need smartphones.
Body Augmentation

Body augmentation is the use of technology to enhance or modify the human body. In our current society, we’re already altering our bodies, including things like the implantation of devices like cochlear implants for hearing or pacemakers for controlling heart function.

As part of the future internet, body augmentation will go further. Mojo Vision is developing AR contact lenses that position micro-LED displays inside the wearer’s eyes. Imagine being able to put in these lenses, and see weather information, chat messages, or AR information about the world around you.

Some companies are pushing body augmentation even further and trying to hook our brains directly to the internet. “Neural VR” combines virtual reality technology with brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) to create immersive, interactive experiences that are directly connected to an individual's nervous system.
With neural VR, users can control and interact with virtual environments and objects using their thoughts rather than traditional input devices such as a keyboard or controller.
Companies like Neurable, Meta, and Elon Musk’s Neuralink project are all working on BCIs that have the potential to revolutionize the way we interact with the future internet.
Bringing the Touch and Sound to the Internet
Developers are also hard at work on devices and wearables that allow us to experience the internet on deeper levels using a full range of senses.

There are already haptic suits like the Teslasuit that can deliver a full-body experience that simulates actual touch, but they’re not currently attainable for most VR users. In the future, haptic devices like gloves will become more accessible for everyday users, and lightweight, wearable haptic patches will deliver a sense of human touch across long distances.

Sound will also play an incredibly important role in the Internet of the future. With spatial audio, we'll be able to enjoy 360-degree sound in our virtual experiences and communications. Companies will also need to give more thought to their sonic branding — the use of music or audio elements to create their own unique, consistent, and memorable audio identity.
But in the end anything could happen as all of this just speculations just like people in past tried to predict trends of the future.
